title: How to sculpt supermodel cheeks for your patients! (It's not JUST about injection technique)
filename: How to sculpt supermodel cheeks for your patients! (It_s not JUST about injection technique).en.vtt
excerpt: Patients will often come and ask you for higher, more defined cheeks. And if you have a look at supermodels, we can see why.

segments.text: Patients will often come and ask you for higher, more defined cheeks. And if you have a look at supermodels, we can see why. If cheeks are dominant in a female face, that means wider than the jawline and well- definfined, where the catch light just frames the eyes, it creates what I call the presentation box for the eyes. This idea that everything is drawn towards the eyes through the framing of catch lights and shadows as you work down the face. And this is where it's really important to see the cheeks as part of the overall context of the face. We simply cannot out compete the lower face by just adding more and more filler in the cheek. Many of the best before and afters that you'll see will use multiple modalities to achieve the ideal outcome. That could mean weight loss or liposuction to remove volume that's competing with the cheeks in the upper face or simply being naturally slim with smaller cheeks that then can be corrected with adding volume. There's now a dramatic contrast between the eye itself and the cheek, which makes the eyes really stand out. As soon as shadows creep in here, or there is a flatter cheek that doesn't catch the light, the eyes actually look less vivid in our mind's eye. So, a catch light comes from a projection underneath. We need to be purposely shifting the shape of the cheek to frame the eyes. In female cheeks, there are three core projections. There's an anterior projection on the front of the cheek. There's the angle of the zygoma.

And then there's the widest point of the cheek here, which is more superior and upward-f facing in females. In men, you often see almost nothing there at all. This is therefore one of the most feminine parts of the face and cannot be overlooked when doing cheek treatments. Notice also in our example of Kate Moss, how the cheek looks separated from the rest of the face, but in the most subtle way possible. There is clearly a separation between the cheek and the jawline, the cheek and the lip, and the cheek and the eye. But the distinction between these structures is very gently faded in youth. As you get older, nasal labial folds and other lines and volume loss make these more harsh. But a beautiful cheek is both distinct but also very gently blended in with the rest of the face. That shadow shouldn't ideally cross beneath a line that runs an inverted line between the corner of the mouth and the midpoint of your ear. And the bit that we can control as injectors is the volume in each part of those components. The typical place that you inject to make a more feminine cheek would start around the defining points. That angle of the zygoma is crucial as is the lateral part of the cheek where the zygoma meets the temple bone. We then want to create a roundness to that shape. So injections that are slightly lateral to the angle of the zygoma are often where we spend most of our time and attention. But never forget that projection of the cheek anteriorly also gives a three-dimensional shape reflecting good prominent cheekbones. So

what are the challenges when treating cheeks? The first thing is if there is already excessive volume or proportion, we shouldn't try and solve problems by adding more. It's quite tempting and many injectors will cross this line at various points where you try and create definition because the underlying problem is excess volume and all that you do is you change the proportion and the patient looks overtreated. So, be careful of patients who have a relatively full cheeks, but they're full above and below as well, because you can't always compensate with more volume. This is where your patient likely needs to lose a little bit of body weight. They are almost certainly already aware of this, and what they are looking for is a kind and supporting person who can do something to help them. Now, maybe if you're focused entirely on aesthetics, you will only focus on injections, but good advice is always worth having. You may produce a leaflet that helps patients lose weight in the most evidence-based way, for example. But later on, you may also be providing weight loss injections or other protocols that help your patients. And this is how facial aesthetics can lead into health and longevity for your

patients. Because ultimately, it'll benefit them in many ways if you solve the underlying problem rather than trying to cover everything up with short-term simple solutions that don't actually solve anything. It's in these situations that I'm reminded constantly that in the long arc of time, the truth is your only friend. You cannot avoid the reality of the situation. If you treat patients who don't need it with filler to try and solve a problem that requires weight loss, you will pay a price. Similarly, if you're not honest with them about why you don't want to treat them, they will also pick up this dishonesty and not want to see you again. The only solution is with love to give them the truth. And this is something that you can practice and get better at. And most patients, if you do it well, they will be grateful for it or at least patient with you, even if they decide to ignore your advice. But at least you have said something that brings integrity to you as the practitioner. Also remember to see your patients as part of a journey. Every consultation does not have to solve every problem, but you can be the start of solving much more important problems years down the line. This is often the

case that little conversations that you think don't mean much turn out to be a turning point for some of your patients. Always empower them with the truth, which will lead to a better result in the long run.
